votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, including grid validation, finding the unique '1', computing its valid neighbors, finding the minimum neighbor, and constructing the expected alternating sequence.

The Python code correctly implements all the constraints and logic from the Coq specification, including the grid properties (size, row length, uniqueness, and range of values), the identification of the cell containing 1, the neighbor calculation, and the construction of the alternating sequence based on the minimum neighbor.

The Python code correctly implements all the constraints and logic defined in the Coq specification, including the grid properties (size, row length, uniqueness, and range of values), the identification of the cell containing 1, the calculation of its minimum neighbor, and the construction of the resulting sequence based on the parity of the index.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. It checks all the same conditions: N >= 2, all rows have length N, all values are unique (NoDup), all values are in range [1, N*N], finds the cell containing 1, computes neighbors using the same boundary conditions (0 < x, x+1 < N, etc.), finds the minimum neighbor, and constructs the answer using the same even/odd pattern. The Coq spec uses Nat.even which returns true for 0, 2, 4... matching Python's i % 2 == 0. The neighbor collection order matches. The only difference is that Python returns False when neighbors is empty, but in a valid grid with N >= 2 and cell containing 1, there will always be at least one neighbor, so this doesn't cause a mismatch.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. It checks all the same conditions: N >= 2, all rows have length N, all values are unique (NoDup), all values are in range [1, N*N], finds the position of 1, computes neighbors correctly using the same boundary conditions (0 < x, x+1 < N, etc.), finds the minimum neighbor, and constructs the expected answer using the same even/odd pattern. The Python code returns True/False which corresponds to whether the Prop is satisfiable for the given inputs, which is the correct interpretation for checking concrete inputs against a spec.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. It checks all the same conditions: N >= 2, all rows have length N, all values are unique (NoDup), all values are in range [1, N*N], finds the cell containing 1, computes neighbors correctly using the same boundary conditions (0 < x, x+1 < N, etc.), finds the minimum neighbor, and constructs the expected answer using the same even/odd pattern (Nat.even i in Coq means i % 2 == 0 in Python). The Python returns True/False based on whether all conditions hold and ans matches the expected result, which corresponds to the Coq Prop being satisfiable for those concrete inputs.

Checks grid is square with N>=2, values unique and in [1..N^2], finds the (unique) 1, collects in-bounds neighbors, uses the minimum neighbor, and builds ans as [1 if even index else mn] for i in 0..k-1—matching the Coq spec’s concrete behavior.

The Python function enforces the same square-grid, uniqueness, and value-range constraints, locates the (unique) cell with value 1, gathers valid orthogonal neighbors exactly as in Coq, takes their minimum, and checks ans alternates 1 and that minimum for k steps, matching the Coq spec on all valid inputs.

The Python function enforces the same grid shape, uniqueness, and value bounds, finds the (unique) cell with value 1, gathers valid neighbors, selects their minimum, and checks ans equals the even/odd pattern over seq 0..k-1. This matches the Coq spec’s concrete behavior.
